subject: Economics
contextNote: JC2 Economics, 24 students. You modelled supply and demand shifts to a new equilibrium using a worked example: a rise in input costs shifts the supply curve left, creating a temporary shortage at the old price, which drives the price up to a new equilibrium (P*, Q*). Students are now 28 minutes into three shift questions. The graphs look correct — but the reasoning behind them is almost entirely invisible.

students.name: Rajan
students.note: All 3 graphs correct and drawn quickly. Minimal labels — no surplus/shortage arrows, no adjustment mechanism shown.

students.name: Nur Atiqah
students.note: Correct shifts drawn for Q1 and Q2. No written explanation, no labels on the adjustment path.

students.name: Wei Jie
students.note: Correct equilibrium outcomes. Has written some working but erases it whenever you approach his desk.

students.name: Siti
students.note: Correct final equilibrium marked on all three graphs. The adjustment process — surplus, shortage, price movement — is completely blank.

ep1.situation: 28 minutes into the lesson. You circulate and the graphs look right — most students have the correct new equilibrium marked. But the reasoning is almost entirely invisible. Rajan has drawn all three graphs correctly and quickly, but his pages show only the final equilibrium point with almost no labels. No surplus or shortage arrows, no explanation of the adjustment mechanism, no policy link. Nur has drawn the supply shift correctly for Q1 and Q2 — the curve is in the right place and the new equilibrium is marked. But she has written nothing. No labels, no explanation, no working. Wei Jie has the correct equilibrium outcomes, but whenever you approach his desk, he erases his working. You can see faint pencil marks where he has written something and rubbed it out. Siti has the correct final equilibrium marked on all three graphs — but the space where the adjustment process should be explained is completely blank. A quick scan of the class: 19 of 24 students have the correct new equilibrium. But only 8 students have labelled the shift, and only 4 have written any explanation of the adjustment mechanism. The room looks productive. The books tell a different story. You have about 17 minutes of lesson time remaining.
ep1.evidence: Most students have correct final equilibria but almost no visible reasoning. The gap is not in accuracy — it is in process visibility. You cannot tell whether students understand the adjustment mechanism (surplus/shortage → price movement → new equilibrium) or are drawing the correct outcome by pattern-matching from the worked example.

choices.label: Two-step equilibrium and adjustment check
choices.elaboration: Stop the class and project a new scenario: a tax is imposed on producers of petrol. Ask two questions in sequence: '(1) Draw the new equilibrium — label the shift, the old price, and the new price.' Then: '(2) Explain why there is a temporary surplus or shortage before the market adjusts. Use the words: surplus, shortage, price, quantity supplied, quantity demanded.' Ask students to write both answers on paper. Use the pattern of responses to see who understands the adjustment mechanism and who is drawing the correct outcome without understanding why.
choices.tradeoff: The two-step structure reveals both the graphical accuracy and the reasoning behind it, giving you whole-class evidence of where the invisible understanding sits. But students who are drawing correctly by pattern-matching may struggle to articulate the mechanism and feel exposed. The public demand for written explanation may embarrass students like Siti who have correct graphs but no visible process.

choices.label: Flex grouping: fast students explain shift logic to slower students
choices.elaboration: Pair students who finished quickly and correctly (like Rajan) with students who are still working or have minimal labels (like Nur). Ask the fast student to explain the shift logic using the stem: 'The supply curve shifts ___ because ___. This creates a temporary ___ at the old price. So the price ___ until the market reaches a new equilibrium at P* and Q*.' The listening student must ask one clarifying question: 'Why does the price move in that direction?' Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one pair to share their explanation with the class.
choices.tradeoff: Peer explanation makes the invisible reasoning visible in a low-stakes way and gives fast finishers a cognitively demanding task — explaining is harder than drawing. But mismatched fluency means some pairs will have one student explaining and one copying rather than both reasoning. Siti may nod along without genuinely engaging with the mechanism.

choices.label: Accountable Talk: connect shift to policy implication
choices.elaboration: Assign shoulder partners and ask them to connect their graph to a policy implication using the stem: 'This shift means ___ because ___. The policy implication is ___. Do you agree?' The listener must respond: 'I agree because ___' or 'I disagree because ___.' Circulate and listen for pairs where the policy connection is genuine versus scripted. After 3 minutes, select two contrasting pairs — one with a nuanced policy link, one with a shallow or incorrect one — and run a brief IRF probe with the whole class: 'What did you notice about these two policy claims? Which reasoning is stronger? Why?'
choices.tradeoff: Accountable Talk pushes students to connect their graphical understanding to real-world application, which may surface reasoning that the graphs alone do not reveal. But students who are drawing correctly without understanding the mechanism may stay hidden in the discussion — they can agree with their partner's policy claim without articulating their own reasoning. Siti may echo her partner without engaging with the mechanism.

A.consequence: The responses come in and the pattern is clear. 19 of 24 students drew the correct new equilibrium for the tax scenario. But on the explanation question, only 11 of 24 wrote any explanation of the adjustment mechanism — and 8 of those wrote 'the price moves right' or 'supply goes down' without explaining the surplus/shortage logic. Nur wrote 'tax up, supply down, price up' — she has the direction right but has not explained why the price rises (the temporary shortage mechanism). Rajan wrote both steps clearly: 'Tax raises production costs → supply shifts left → at old price, Qs < Qd → temporary shortage → price rises to P*.' His explanation is complete and precise. Wei Jie erased his explanation before showing you — you can see faint marks where he had written something. Siti wrote only the outcome: 'New equilibrium at higher price and lower quantity.' The adjustment mechanism is completely absent. The evidence is now clear: correct graphs are masking a wide range of invisible understanding, from Rajan's complete mechanism to Siti's outcome-only thinking. 15 minutes remain.

choices.label: Process feedback: model the surplus/shortage adjustment
choices.elaboration: Address the invisible mechanism directly with the whole class. Write the adjustment chain on the board step by step: 'Step 1: Tax → production costs rise → supply curve shifts left. Step 2: At the old price P₀, quantity supplied falls but quantity demanded stays the same → temporary shortage (Qd > Qs). Step 3: Shortage puts upward pressure on price → price rises. Step 4: As price rises, Qd falls and Qs rises until Qd = Qs at the new equilibrium P*, Q*.' Ask all students to re-label their Q1 graph using this chain, adding arrows and labels for each step. Then ask them to write one sentence explaining why the price rises rather than falls.
choices.tradeoff: The process feedback makes the adjustment mechanism explicit for everyone and gives students a clear chain to follow. It will reach the 13 students who had the direction right but not the mechanism. But it treats the class as uniform and shifts back to teacher-led instruction. Rajan, who already understood the full mechanism, is under-challenged. Siti may re-label her graph correctly without building genuine understanding of why the price moves.

choices.label: AQS peer feedback on graphs and explanations
choices.elaboration: Use the diagnostic evidence to form pairs: students who wrote a complete explanation (like Rajan) are paired with students who had the direction right but not the mechanism (like Nur) or who wrote only the outcome (like Siti). The complete-explanation student gives AQS feedback on their partner's graph: Affirm what is correct ('Your shift is in the right direction — supply moves left'), Question what is missing ('Why does the price rise? What happens at the old price before the market adjusts?'), Suggest what to add ('Try labelling the temporary shortage at P₀ — that is what drives the price up'). Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one pair to share their AQS exchange.
choices.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback is more personalised than a whole-class reteach and activates the expertise already in the room. Nur, who has the direction right but not the mechanism, may benefit from having the shortage logic explained by a peer in conversational terms. But Wei Jie erased his explanation, so his partner has nothing to give feedback on — the partner will end up explaining from scratch. Siti's blank process gives her partner nothing to affirm, and the AQS structure breaks down.

B.consequence: The pairs begin working and the room shifts from quiet to active. Rajan explains the shift logic to Nur with precision: 'The supply curve shifts left because the tax raises production costs. At the old price, quantity supplied is now less than quantity demanded — that is a temporary shortage. The shortage pushes the price up until the market clears at the new equilibrium.' Nur listens, nods, and immediately adds labels to her graph — surplus/shortage arrows, price movement arrow, new equilibrium label. She says: 'Oh — I had the right graph but I didn't know why the price moved.' Wei Jie explains partially to Siti: 'The curve shifts left, so the price goes up.' Siti copies his labels onto her graph. But Wei Jie cannot explain the shortage mechanism — he knows the outcome but not the path. Siti's labels are now correct, but she has copied them without understanding. You circulate and hear the pattern: 7 pairs have genuine depth transfer, with the listening student asking clarifying questions and adding reasoning to their graph. 5 pairs are superficial — one student explaining the outcome, the other copying the labels. The room is more active than before, but the depth of understanding is uneven. 16 minutes remain.

choices.label: IRF probe: why does the price move in that direction?
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and select two pairs who explained well. Ask the whole class: 'Why does the supply curve shift left — not right? And what happens at the old price before the market adjusts?' Take the first response (Initiation). Then ask a second student: 'Can you build on that? What is the name for what happens at the old price?' (Response). Then ask a third student: 'Do you agree? Is there anything you would add or correct?' (Feedback). Use the IRF chain to build a complete, class-constructed explanation of the adjustment mechanism. Write the final chain on the board.
choices.tradeoff: The IRF probe makes the adjustment mechanism visible and public after the peer explanation phase. It deepens the reasoning of the 7 pairs who were explaining genuinely and gives the whole class a shared, class-built explanation. But it exposes the 5 superficial pairs — students who were copying labels rather than reasoning may be called on and unable to answer. Siti, who copied Wei Jie's labels without understanding, may freeze if asked to explain the mechanism.

choices.label: Solo policy application: new scenario, individual response
choices.elaboration: Give a new scenario: a subsidy is given to producers of solar panels. Ask each student to work individually: '(1) Draw the new equilibrium — label the shift, the surplus or shortage at the old price, and the price movement. (2) Write one policy recommendation based on your graph, with a reason.' Give 4 minutes, then collect the responses. Scan them to see who can transfer the mechanism independently after the peer explanation.
choices.tradeoff: Individual application tests whether the peer explanation has built genuine understanding or just transferred labels. Rajan and Nur, who had a genuine exchange, are likely ready. But the move to solo work removes the support of the peer explanation for students who were copying. Siti, who copied Wei Jie's labels without understanding the mechanism, will likely stall at the policy step. You will have clean evidence — but less time to respond.

C.consequence: The pairs begin talking and the room gets louder. Rajan's pair produces a nuanced policy link: 'The tax shifts supply left, creating a shortage at the old price. The price rises to P*. Policy implication: the tax burden is shared between producers and consumers — how much depends on the elasticity of demand.' His partner agrees and adds a point about tax incidence. Nur says to her partner: 'I agree, it works — the price goes up.' Her partner nods. No deeper reasoning emerges. Wei Jie asks his partner: 'Is there a shortage or a surplus?' — he understands the concept but cannot connect it to the direction of price movement. Siti echoes her partner's policy claim word for word. You circulate and hear the pattern: 5 pairs have deep policy links with genuine mechanism reasoning. 11 pairs are procedural — they have the correct policy direction but no mechanism explanation. 8 pairs are echoing or agreeing without reasoning. The talk has partially revealed the invisible understanding, but the adjustment mechanism remains unexplained for most students. 15 minutes remain.

choices.label: AQS gallery critique on policy claims
choices.elaboration: Ask each pair to write their policy claim on a sticky note in one sentence: 'This shift means ___ because ___. Policy implication: ___.' Post four notes on the board: one with a complete mechanism and policy link, one with the correct policy direction but no mechanism, one with an incorrect mechanism, one with only 'I agree.' Run a whole-class AQS session on the 'I agree' note: Affirm what they attempted ('They identified the correct policy direction'), Question what is missing ('Why does the price rise? What is the mechanism that connects the shift to the policy implication?'), Suggest how to strengthen it ('Try adding: at the old price, there is a temporary ___ which drives the price ___'). Ask all pairs to revise their sticky notes using the AQS feedback.
choices.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique makes the difference between a complete mechanism explanation and a shallow policy claim visible to the whole class. It gives the procedural pairs a concrete model of what genuine reasoning looks like. But the pairs whose notes are displayed may feel exposed. Siti's pair will likely produce an 'I agree' note, and the AQS feedback may feel abstract to students who have not yet articulated the mechanism.

choices.label: Written logic chains: shift → equilibrium → policy
choices.elaboration: Stop the pair talk and ask all students to work individually. Write on the board: 'Write three steps for the tax scenario: Step 1: The shift is ___ because ___. Step 2: At the old price, there is a temporary ___ because ___. The price then ___ until ___. Step 3: The policy implication is ___ because ___.' Give 4 minutes, then collect the responses. Scan them to see who can articulate the full mechanism in writing.
choices.tradeoff: Written logic chains force the invisible reasoning into a visible, structured form and give you clean individual evidence of each student's understanding of the mechanism. But the move to individual writing kills the emerging talk momentum. Siti, who was echoing her partner throughout the discussion, will likely complete Step 1 and stall at Step 2 — the mechanism step — revealing that the pair talk did not help her build the reasoning.

A1.outcome: You write the four-step adjustment chain on the board and ask all students to re-label their Q1 graph. The room goes quiet as students write. Nur looks at the board and immediately adds the shortage arrow and the price movement label to her graph. She says quietly: 'Oh — I had the right graph but I didn't know the shortage was the reason the price moved.' Wei Jie re-labels his graph carefully, copying each step from the board. He does not erase this time — the board model gives him permission to show his working. Rajan re-labels his graph compliantly but his body language shows disengagement — he already understood the mechanism and the reteach does not challenge him. He adds a note in the margin: 'Speed of adjustment depends on elasticity.' Siti copies the board labels onto her graph line by line. When you circulate and ask her 'Why does the price rise rather than fall?', she points to the board and says 'Because of the shortage.' Her answer is correct, but she is reading from the board rather than reasoning independently. On a follow-up check, 21 of 24 students now have labelled graphs with the adjustment mechanism, up from 8. The process feedback has made the mechanism visible for almost everyone. But the understanding is uniform — you cannot tell who genuinely understands and who has copied the labels. Rajan is under-challenged. Siti can label correctly but cannot explain without the board. 12 minutes remain.
A1.tradeoff: The process feedback is efficient and reaches the majority of students who had the direction right but not the mechanism. It is task-level feedback (Hattie & Timperley) — you explained what the correct adjustment chain looks like and why. Nur and Wei Jie made clear gains. But the reteach homogenises the variety of understanding in the room and may have replaced intuitive reasoning with labelling compliance. Rajan is under-challenged and Siti is dependent on the board model.
A1.reflection:
Why did you choose whole-class process feedback over peer feedback? What were you prioritising — efficiency, clarity, or equity?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? 21 students now have labelled graphs, but Rajan is disengaged and Siti is copying rather than reasoning. What does this tell you about the difference between correct labelling and genuine understanding of the mechanism?

How do you design tasks that make genuine understanding of economic mechanisms visible, rather than just correct graph labelling?

A2.outcome: You pair Rajan with Nur and ask Rajan to give AQS feedback on Nur's graph. Rajan looks at Nur's graph and begins: 'Your shift is in the right direction — supply moves left, that is correct. But you haven't labelled the shortage. At the old price P₀, quantity supplied is now less than quantity demanded — that is a temporary shortage. That shortage is what drives the price up to P*.' Nur listens, nods, and adds the shortage label and price arrow to her graph. She says: 'Oh — I had the mechanism right in my head but I didn't know I needed to show it.' Wei Jie is paired with a student who wrote a complete explanation. His partner asks: 'Why did you erase your working?' Wei Jie says: 'I wasn't sure it was right.' His partner says: 'Your graph is correct — write the mechanism down.' Wei Jie rewrites his explanation with more confidence. Siti is paired with a student who can explain the outcome but not the mechanism. Her partner says: 'The price goes up because supply goes down.' Siti copies this onto her graph. When you ask her 'What happens at the old price before the market adjusts?', she says 'I don't know.' On a follow-up check, 17 of 24 students now have the mechanism labelled, up from 8. The peer feedback has activated genuine reasoning for Nur and Wei Jie. But Siti's partner could not explain the shortage mechanism, so Siti remains dependent on copying. Rajan is giving rather than receiving. 12 minutes remain.
A2.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback is more personalised than a whole-class reteach and activates the expertise in the room. Nur and Wei Jie made real gains through structured peer exchange — the feedback was specific and connected to their actual graphs. But the quality of peer explanation varies. Siti's partner could not explain the shortage mechanism, so Siti remains dependent on copying. Rajan is under-challenged — giving feedback is not the same as being challenged.
A2.reflection:
Why did you choose AQS peer feedback over whole-class process feedback? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Nur and Wei Jie made genuine gains, but Siti is still copying and Rajan is under-challenged. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive peer feedback when understanding is invisible?

How do you ensure that the student giving AQS feedback is also learning — not just explaining what they already know?

B1.outcome: You pause the class and run the IRF probe. You ask: 'Why does the supply curve shift left — not right? And what happens at the old price before the market adjusts?' A student from a strong pair responds (Initiation): 'The tax raises production costs, so producers supply less at every price — the curve shifts left.' You ask a second student to build on this (Response): 'At the old price P₀, quantity supplied is now less than quantity demanded — there is a temporary shortage.' A third student evaluates (Feedback): 'Yes, and the shortage puts upward pressure on price — buyers compete for the limited supply, so the price rises until the market clears at P*.' The IRF chain produces a complete, class-constructed explanation of the adjustment mechanism. You write it on the board. Nur listens carefully and adds the shortage label to her graph. Wei Jie follows the chain and nods — the class-built explanation has given him the language he was missing. Rajan listens with interest — the chain has articulated the mechanism more precisely than he had in his own notes. Siti watches but does not contribute. When you ask her 'Does that make sense?', she nods quickly but does not speak. Her graph still has no shortage label. On a follow-up check, 18 of 24 students can now explain the adjustment mechanism, up from 11. 12 minutes remain.
B1.tradeoff: The IRF probe makes the adjustment mechanism visible and public after the peer explanation phase. It leverages the genuine reasoning from the 7 strong pairs and builds a shared explanation that is richer than any single student would produce. Rajan was unexpectedly engaged by the precision of the class-built chain. But the probe exposed the 5 superficial pairs — students who were copying labels rather than reasoning. Siti did not contribute and the chain did not give her an entry point into the mechanism.
B1.reflection:
Why did you choose to run an IRF probe after the peer explanation rather than moving directly to individual application? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? The chain produced strong reasoning from the middle group, but Siti remained passive and the superficial pairs were exposed. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for whole-class probing to include all students?

How do you design peer explanation tasks so that both the explainer and the listener are reasoning — not just one teaching and one copying?

B2.outcome: You give the subsidy scenario and ask students to work individually. Rajan draws a clear diagram with full labels — supply shifts right, temporary surplus at old price, price falls to new equilibrium — and writes a nuanced policy recommendation: 'Subsidy lowers production costs → supply shifts right → surplus at old price → price falls → quantity demanded rises. Policy: effective for increasing consumption of merit goods, but cost to government depends on elasticity.' His response shows complete understanding and policy application. Nur draws the shift correctly and labels the surplus and price movement. Her policy recommendation is brief but accurate: 'Price falls, quantity rises — good for consumers.' Wei Jie draws the shift correctly but cannot write the policy implication — he leaves it blank. Siti draws the supply curve shifting right but does not label the surplus or the price movement. Her policy space is blank. The individual application has revealed a clear split: Rajan and Nur have genuine understanding of the mechanism; Wei Jie has the graphical understanding but not the policy connection; Siti has the direction right but not the mechanism or the policy. You now have clean evidence — but 12 minutes remain and two students need significant support.
B2.tradeoff: Individual application tests whether the peer explanation has built genuine understanding or just transferred labels. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine understanding — they could draw the mechanism and connect it to policy independently. But Wei Jie and Siti stalled at different points. The move to solo work removed the support of the peer explanation before Wei Jie and Siti had fully internalised the mechanism. You have clear evidence of the gap — but limited time to address it.
B2.reflection:
Why did you choose to move from peer explanation to individual application? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur succeeded, but Wei Jie cannot connect to policy and Siti cannot explain the mechanism. What does this tell you about the timing of individual application after peer explanation?

How do you design peer explanation tasks so that both the explainer and the listener are ready for independent application — not just one of them?

C1.outcome: Four sticky notes are posted on the board. You run the AQS session on the 'I agree' note. Several students affirm: 'They identified the correct policy direction.' You write this on the board. Then you ask: 'What should we question?' Nur raises her hand: 'Why does the price rise? What is the mechanism that connects the shift to the policy implication?' You write: 'Question: What is the mechanism? What happens at the old price?' A third student suggests: 'Add: at the old price, there is a temporary shortage which drives the price up.' You write this on the board. The AQS feedback is now complete and visible. You ask all pairs to revise their sticky notes. Several procedural pairs begin adding mechanism language to their notes for the first time. Wei Jie's pair adds the shortage step and connects it to the policy implication. Nur's pair revises their note to include the surplus/shortage mechanism. Siti's pair produces a revised note that still has no mechanism — they write 'price goes up because supply goes down' but cannot explain the shortage logic. When you circulate and ask Siti's pair 'What happens at the old price?', they look at the board and copy 'temporary shortage' without being able to explain it. 12 minutes remain.
C1.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique makes the difference between a complete mechanism explanation and a shallow policy claim visible to the whole class. It gave the procedural pairs a concrete model of what genuine reasoning looks like, and several pairs added mechanism language to their notes for the first time. But Siti's pair remains stuck — they can see what is missing but cannot produce the reasoning. The AQS feedback served the middle group but did not give Siti's pair an entry point into the mechanism.
C1.reflection:
Why did you choose to use the gallery talk output as the basis for AQS feedback? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Several pairs added mechanism reasoning for the first time, but Siti's pair remains stuck. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for AQS feedback to activate new reasoning rather than just model it?

How do you design Accountable Talk tasks so that the mechanism is built through the discussion rather than just confirmed by the feedback?

C2.outcome: The room goes quiet as students write their three-step logic chains. Rajan writes a complete, detailed chain: 'Step 1: Tax raises production costs → supply shifts left. Step 2: At old price P₀, Qs < Qd → temporary shortage → price rises until Qd = Qs at P*, Q*. Step 3: Tax burden shared between producers and consumers — ratio depends on PED.' His chain shows complete mechanism understanding and policy application. Nur writes Steps 1 and 2 correctly — she has the shortage mechanism — but her Step 3 is thin: 'Price rises, consumers pay more.' Wei Jie writes Step 1 correctly and begins Step 2: 'At the old price, there is a temporary...' and stops. He cannot complete the mechanism in writing. Siti writes Step 1 and leaves Steps 2 and 3 blank. When you circulate and ask her 'What happens at the old price?', she says 'I don't know.' The logic chains have given you the clearest, most honest picture of each student's understanding. Rajan has complete mechanism and policy reasoning. Nur has the mechanism but thin policy. Wei Jie has the direction but not the mechanism in writing. Siti has the shift but nothing beyond it. The pair talk did not build the mechanism understanding for most students — it confirmed what they already knew. 12 minutes remain.
C2.tradeoff: Written logic chains are the most honest measure of invisible understanding — they force the reasoning into a visible, structured form and give you clean individual evidence. Rajan's chain shows complete understanding. But the move to individual writing revealed that the pair talk did not build the mechanism for Wei Jie or Siti — it confirmed their existing understanding rather than extending it. The talk was social, not cognitive, for most pairs.
C2.reflection:
Why did you choose to move from pair talk to written logic chains? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan's chain shows complete understanding, but Wei Jie and Siti cannot articulate the mechanism. What does this tell you about when pair talk builds genuine mechanism understanding versus borrowed policy language?

What is the relationship between talk and writing in building understanding of economic mechanisms? How do you design a lesson so that the talk produces the reasoning that the writing then captures — rather than revealing that the talk produced nothing new?
